---
title: "A review of the toxicological effects and allergenic potential of emerging alternative protein sources"
author: "Matilde Milana et al."
year: 2025
doi: "10.1111/1541-4337.70123"
journal:
   name: "Comprehensive Reviews in Food Science and Food Safety"
volume: "24:e70123"
pmid: "DON'T KNOW"
access: "Paywalled — Wiley Online Library. PDF accessed via institutional download (Wageningen University and Research, dated 27/01/2025)."
source_quality: "Full text read from PDF."
---

# A review of the toxicological effects and allergenic potential of emerging alternative protein sources

**Authors:** Matilde Milana et al. (full author list: DON'T KNOW from PDF header)
**Year:** 2025 (received Oct 2024, revised Jan 2025, accepted Jan 2025)
**DOI:** 10.1111/1541-4337.70123
**Journal:** Comprehensive Reviews in Food Science and Food Safety, 24:e70123

## Why this paper

Brought in specifically for mung bean (*Vigna radiata*) and black gram (*Vigna mungo*) allergenicity data. These two legumes are major protein sources in Indian diets — black gram is used in dal makhani, idli/dosa batter, and papad; mung bean in moong dal, chaat, and sprouts. The paper is a broad review of alternative proteins, but its mung bean and black gram sections provide the most detailed allergen molecular characterisation in our paper set.

## Extracted points — mung bean section

> **Source quality note:** Full text read from PDF. All points below are from the paper. Text in quotation marks is taken directly. Table data for cross-reactivity was garbled in PDF extraction (rotated text); specific cross-reactivity entries are reconstructed from the prose sections. Where a reference number is mentioned, it refers to citations within the paper.

### Mung bean IUIS-named allergens

The paper lists 6 IUIS-named mung bean allergens (Vig r series):

| IUIS name | Molecular weight | Protein family | Notes |
|-----------|-----------------|----------------|-------|
| Vig r1 | 16 kDa | PR-10 / Bet v1-homologous | Identified in seedlings; cross-reacts with birch pollen allergen Bet v1; high sequence homology with PR-10 proteins from yellow lupine |
| Vig r2 | 52 kDa | Vicilin-type seed storage protein | Cross-reacts with soybean, lentil, pea, peanut, lupine |
| Vig r3 | 50 kDa | Vicilin | Later renamed Vig r2.0201 — DON'T KNOW if this represents a separate allergen or a reclassification |
| Vig r4 | 30 kDa | Seed albumin | — |
| Vig r5 | 18 kDa | Fragment of Vig r2 | — |
| Vig r6 | 18 kDa | Cytokinin-binding protein / Bet v1-homologous | Cross-reacts with birch pollen allergen Bet v1 |

> Note: Vig r2 through Vig r5 share "high sequence similarity with allergens from soybean, lentil, pea, peanut, and lupine, suggesting the potential for cross-reactivity." — [FULL TEXT, direct quote]

### Mung bean — additional allergen data

1. "Vig r1 (identified in seedlings) and Vig r6 are birch pollen allergen (Bet v1)-homologous and can induce IgE-mediated allergenic cross-reactions in birch pollen allergen-sensitized patients." — [FULL TEXT, direct quote, citing Guhsl et al. 2014; Mittag et al. 2005]
   For us: Cross-reactions with birch pollen allergen may cause pollen-food allergy syndrome in birch-sensitised patients eating mung bean. DON'T KNOW if birch pollen is a significant sensitiser in India.

2. Lipid transfer proteins (LTPs) are also present in mung bean. Mung bean LTPs "are closely related to allergenic LTPs from peanuts, peach and green beans and share >60% homology with LTPs from lentils, beans, peanuts, strawberries and apples." — [FULL TEXT, direct quote, citing Mishra & Kumar 2021]
   For us: LTP cross-reactivity creates a cluster of potentially co-reactive foods. LTPs are heat-stable and digestion-resistant, which makes them relevant for labelling.

3. Mung bean proteins "produced positive SPTs and immunological responses in peanut-allergic patients and showed cross-reactivity with soybeans." — [FULL TEXT, paraphrase from Cho et al. 2017; Jensen et al. 2008]

4. Allergen properties (from general Vig r2–r5 description): "resistance to digestion" and "ability to elicit IgE-mediated allergenic responses in human and experimental animals." — [FULL TEXT, direct quote]
   For us: Processing-resistant allergens are harder to eliminate through cooking. This is relevant for both consumer guidance and food labelling.

## Extracted points — black gram section

### Black gram IUIS-named allergens

Only **one** IUIS-named allergen for black gram (*Vigna mungo*):

| IUIS name | Molecular weight | Biochemical identity | Notes |
|-----------|-----------------|---------------------|-------|
| 28 kDa Vig m | 28 kDa | Sequence homology to rho-specific inhibitor of transcription termination (peanut) | Only characterised allergen with IUIS designation |

Note: The paper uses "28 kDa Vig m" as the IUIS reference but the full IUIS name format would be Vig m 1 — DON'T KNOW if the paper names it Vig m 1 explicitly.

1. "Multiple IgE-binding proteins have been identified; however, to date, the only allergen identified and characterized is the 28 kDa Vig m." — [FULL TEXT, direct quote]
   Source cited: Verma et al. 2012, 2013.

2. The 28 kDa Vig m "exhibited allergen-like properties (i.e., resistance to digestion) and sequence homology to the rho-specific inhibitor of transcription termination of peanut." — [FULL TEXT, direct quote]
   For us: Resistance to digestion confirms this allergen persists through normal digestive processing.

### Black gram — cross-reactivity

3. "Black gram can cross-react with legumes (i.e., faba bean, lentil, lima bean, and pea)." — [FULL TEXT, direct quote, citing Arora et al. 2021; Kumar et al. 2014; Verma et al. 2013]

4. Black gram also cross-reacts with the plant *Prosopis juliflora* (a pollen allergen common in India) — described as "Pollen Food Allergy Syndrome." — [FULL TEXT, citing Arora et al. 2021]
   For us: Pollen-food allergy syndrome means patients sensitised to *Prosopis juliflora* pollen (which is environmentally prevalent in India) may also react to black gram. This is a clinically distinct mechanism from direct food sensitisation.

## Cross-reactivity context from Table in paper

The paper includes a cross-reactivity table for all emerging alternative proteins. From the prose sections confirmed for mung bean:
- Mung bean cross-reacts with: peanut (SPT and immunological responses confirmed), soybean (cross-reactivity confirmed), lentils, beans, strawberries, apples (LTP homology >60%)
- Black gram cross-reacts with: faba bean, lentil, lima bean, pea (direct statement); Prosopis juliflora pollen (PFAS mechanism)

Note: The full table in the paper also lists mung bean cross-reactivity symbols for multiple additional foods (the row in the table). The exact entries from the table are DON'T KNOW due to rotated text extraction failure.

## Gaps noted in this paper

- "Few studies were found" for quinoa and hemp seeds — similar data scarcity likely applies to black gram in non-Indian literature
- India-specific prevalence data for mung bean or black gram allergy is not provided in this paper — molecular characterisation only
- DON'T KNOW whether the paper discusses processing effects specifically for black gram (the extraction was partial for this section)

## What this paper does not address

- Population prevalence figures for mung bean or black gram allergy in India
- Clinical case series
- Regulatory classification of mung bean or black gram under any national allergen list
